coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical miR-155 nanocomposite biosensor based on functionalized/conjugated graphene materials and gold nanostars
coredata.dc:creator: Khosravi, Fatemeh
Rahaie, Mahdi
Ghaani, Mohammad Reza
Azimzadeh, Mostafa
Mostafavi, Ebrahim
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical nanobiosensors have revolutionized the field of medical diagnosis owing to the unique properties of the utilized nanomaterial that can boost the sensitivity of these devices. The higher sensitivity is especially critical when a small sample containing little quantity of the biomarker, miRNAs for instance, should be quantified for medical diagnosis. Here, a novel sensitive electrochemical nanobiosensor for miR-155, an early breast cancer detection biomarker, is introduced. A combination of reduced graphene oxide modified with nickel-iron (Fe-Ni@rGO), silver-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQD-Ag), and gold nanostars (GNS) were used. This novel and advanced nanocomposite expanded the surface area and conductivity of the electrode surface to remarkably enhance the sensitivity of the nanobiosensor. A series of systematic characterization, both electrochemical and microscopic, were employed to assess the nanocomposite features using SEM, EDS, TEM, AFM, CV, and EIS. Thiolated single-stranded capture probes were immobilized on the GNS to selectively capture the target miR-155, and hematoxylin was the electrochemical label. The results showed high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 20.2 aM and a wide dynamic range of 0.05 fM-50.0 pM, which is superior to most of the previously reported miRNA nanobiosensors. The specificity was suitable towards the miR-155 compared to its one- and three-base mismatched oligos, a non-complementary and a mixture of the target with all the other non-complementary. Finally, the results of the real sample study in the serum environment revealed a very low interference in measurements which, along with the high sensitivity and selectivity, raises the possibility of using these nanobiosensors as promising candidates for breast cancer detection/screening applications in the future.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of components and working mechanism of the developed electrochemical nanobiosensor of miR-155.

Optimization of the nanobiosensor parameters on the conductivity of the working electrode by comparing the peak current of cyclic voltammograms measured in the 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl. (A) Evaluating the effect of Fe-Ni@rGO concentrations (left); GQDs concentrations (middle); and GNSs concentrations (right), on peak currents; (B) The effect of different methods of electrode's surface treatment using nanomaterials on peak currents, and (C) The effect of concentrations of thiolated capture SS-Probes on peak currents. Data are presented as mean ± SD (p < 0.05: *, p < 0.01: **, p < 0.001: ***, and p < 0.0001: ****, n = 3).

Results of (A) CV and (B) EIS characterization in the 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl. In both methods, the curves of the electrode with different modifications are named as follows: a) bare GCE, b) GCE after modification with the nanocomposite, c) nano-modified electrode after self-assembling of DNA SS-Probes on the nanocomposite layer (via 5′ end modification over the gold and silver nanoparticles), d) after hybridization of the target miR-155 with the self-assembled DNA probes, e) after accumulation of hematoxylin on the hybridized target miR-155 and DNA probes.

Characterization of the nanomaterials and the working electrode surface of the developed nanobiosensor. (A) TEM image of the GNS; (B) FT-IR spectroscopy of the Fe-Ni@rGO, GQDs, GNSs and their combination; (C) SEM image and (E) EDS analysis of the GCE electrode modified with the Fe-Ni@rGO; (D) SEM image and (F) EDS analysis of the GCE electrode modified with the combination of Fe-Ni@rGO, GQDs, and GNSs; and (G) AFM analysis of the different electrode modification steps of the nano-modified electrode (I), capture probe modified-nanomaterials electrode (single-stranded oligonucleotides) (II) and the hybridized electrode-nanomaterials (double-stranded oligonucleotides) (III).

The Selectivity and analytical performance of the developed electrochemical nanobiosensor. (A) The peak current and (B) DPV curves of the different target oligos measured by the nanobiosensor; (C) The peak current and (D) DPV curves of the different concentrations of the target miR-155 measured by the nanobiosensor; (E) The Calibration curve of the nanobiosensor by plotting the concentrations of the target miR-155 versus their respecting DPV peak currents. Data are presented as mean ± SD (p < 0.05: *, p < 0.01: **, and p < 0.001: ***, n = 3). *Abbreviations: Cp: Capture probe; M1: one-base different miR-155; M3: three-base different miR-155; NC: non-complementary oligo; Mixture: mixing the target miR-155, M1, M3, and NC; No hematoxylin: DPV of target miR-155 without adding hematoxylin as a label.

Study of the nanobiosensor function in serum samples. (A) Comparison of the nanobiosensor function in PBS buffer versus serum environment; (B) DPV curves of the nanobiosensor for different concentrations of the target miR-155 spiked in the human serum sample (ns=not significant, n = 3).

Molecular docking results. (A) ligand/receptor interaction for the best three scoring complexes single-stranded (SS1, SS2, and SS3) and double-stranded (DS1, DS2, and DS3) oligos; (B) Results of molecular dynamics simulation of RSV-DNA interaction with preferred binding positions for the first 20 conformations over-tested 1000 random configurations which are overlapped in the image (SS4 for single-stranded and DS4 for double-stranded); (C) Docking scores exported for the first five complexes with the lowest binding energy for single- and double-stranded oligos; (D) Graph for comparison of single-stranded and double-stranded oligos according to their binding energy.
Comparison of the developed nanobiosensor features with previously published miRNA nanobiosensors (electrochemical and non-electrochemical methods).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical nanobiosensors have revolutionized the field of medical diagnosis owing to the unique properties of the utilized nanomaterial that can boost the sensitivity of these devices. The higher sensitivity is especially critical when a small sample containing little quantity of the biomarker, miRNAs for instance, should be quantified for medical diagnosis. Here, a novel sensitive electrochemical nanobiosensor for miR-155, an early breast cancer detection biomarker, is introduced. A combination of reduced graphene oxide modified with nickel-iron (Fe-Ni@rGO), silver-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQD-Ag), and gold nanostars (GNS) were used. This novel and advanced nanocomposite expanded the surface area and conductivity of the electrode surface to remarkably enhance the sensitivity of the nanobiosensor. A series of systematic characterization, both electrochemical and microscopic, were employed to assess the nanocomposite features using SEM, EDS, TEM, AFM, CV, and EIS. Thiolated single-stranded capture probes were immobilized on the GNS to selectively capture the target miR-155, and hematoxylin was the electrochemical label. The results showed high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 20.2 aM and a wide dynamic range of 0.05 fM-50.0 pM, which is superior to most of the previously reported miRNA nanobiosensors. The specificity was suitable towards the miR-155 compared to its one- and three-base mismatched oligos, a non-complementary and a mixture of the target with all the other non-complementary. Finally, the results of the real sample study in the serum environment revealed a very low interference in measurements which, along with the high sensitivity and selectivity, raises the possibility of using these nanobiosensors as promising candidates for breast cancer detection/screening applications in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Applications of nanomaterials in biosensors, called nanobiosensors, have been changing the diagnostic fields in medical and biological studies over the past two decades. Different nanomaterials and nanostructures with various shapes, surface chemistry, and other special features have been used mainly to enhance the sensitivity of the detection process [1]. This is especially important in electrochemical nanobiosensors, where higher sensitivity and selectivity, facile fabrication, and portability are key factors [2]. The simultaneous implementation of nanomaterials alongside other sensitivity enhancement strategies such as isothermal amplification methods, CRISPR (clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats)-based detection microfluidic sample manipulation, made electrochemical biosensors promising candidates in medical diagnostics, and this has constantly been growing over the past decade [3–9]. The future applications of biosensors in the medical diagnosis market mainly rely on developing low-cost, sensitive, selective and easy-to-use sensors, which can be achieved through using advanced technologies such as nanotechnology, artificial intelligence, wearables, etc [10–13].

Electrochemical nanobiosensors can detect/quantify various biomolecules, namely biomarkers that can report a disease or situation in our body ranging from DNA to proteins. Biomarkers can be acquired from any source and sample (from saliva to blood); however, most recent studies look for less-invasive sampling methods [6,14,15]. MicroRNAs (miRNAs), small non-coding RNA molecules that perform signaling and regulate gene expression, have been used as medical biomarkers over the past 15 years. Some miRNAs can be found in the blood circulation, and it was found that these types of miRNAs are reliable and non-invasive samples to be assessed to detect many diseases, including cancers [16–18]. MicroRNA-155 (miR-155) is proved to be a reliable circulating biomarker to be quantified for breast cancer detection in the blood (serum or plasma) of the patient compared to healthy controls [19,20]. This is especially important because according to the 2020 cancer statistics of the USA, breast cancer is the most common type with new cases (around 30% of all cases) and the second deadliest cancer (around 15% of all cases) among women [21,22].

The quantification of miRNAs in the bloodstream for early breast cancer detection is hard to perform because they are available in small quantities; therefore, advanced methods such as deep sequencing and Real-Time PCR (RT-PCR) are used as conventional miRNA quantification methods. However, these methods are not free of drawbacks and have limitations like high costs, big instruments, and the need for experienced operators. Different nanobiosensors with various transducers have been introduced to overcome those limitations, such as colorimetric, florescent, surface plasmon resonance (SPR), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical nanobiosensors, among which the electrochemical nanobiosensors are the most extensively used strategy [23–25].

Electrochemical nanobiosensors for miRNA quantification have used different nanoparticles and nanostructure or in hybridization with other types of nanomaterials (nanohybrids) or composite with polymers (nanocomposite) to enhance the sensitivity of the target miRNA quantification for detection of many diseases [3,5,26,27]. Previously we proved the impact of nanomaterials in quantifying miR-155 by developing two electrochemical sensing strategies for miRNA detection. We used nanocomposite of gold nanorods and graphene oxide and compared it with a biosensor without using any nanomaterial. The results revealed that the sensitivity of the nanobiosensor was around six times higher, and a very lower limit of detection was achieved when compared to biosensors without nanomaterials [23,28]. Moreover, other types of nanomaterials, hybrid nanomaterials, and nanocomposites have been used by other researchers, including quantum dots, magnetic iron oxide, graphene, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, metal nanoparticles (gold, silver, platinum, etc.), DNA nanostructures and their combination with other nanomaterials or polymers [26,28–33].

Different shapes of gold nanoparticles proved to have different physical and chemical properties such as surface area and electrical conductivity, which are very important in electrochemical sensors. For example, shapes like spherical, rod, wire, urchin, cube, and core-shell have been reported in the electrochemical biosensors [34–36]. However, gold nanostars (GNS) have been used in a few electrochemical sensors to enhance the electron transfer rate as well as electrochemical responses in a few sensors for DNA [37], hydrogen peroxide [38], chromium [39], arsenic, mercury, and lead [40].

Graphene derivatives such as pristine graphene, graphene oxide, and chemically or electrochemically reduced graphene oxide have been used widely in electrochemical biosensors according to their remarkable properties [41]. However, chemically modified or functionalized graphene and graphene oxide have recently been more frequently used because their properties can be enhanced by adding functional groups and doping metals to the sheet surface or edges [42–44]. Reduced graphene oxide modified with nickel and iron (Fe-Ni@rGO), which are GO sheets loaded by Fe and Ni, is more stable and conductive than regular GO and rGO. For instance, this formulation has been used to detect drugs [45] and oxygen reduction reactions [46]. In addition to these nanomaterials, the use of quantum dots (QDs) is also rising due to their incomparable optical, electrical, and magnetic properties, and they have been frequently used in the development of electrochemical nanobiosensors [43,47–50]. Silver-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQD-Ag) have been used in a few electrochemical studies for the detection of tuberculosis [51], Escherichia coli [52], and nitroaromatic compounds [53].

In this study, we have developed an electrochemical nanobiosensor by modifying the glassy carbon electrode with a novel composite containing a combination of three promising nanomaterials (the rationale for using each nanomaterial was discussed above), including Fe-Ni@rGO, GQD-Ag, and GNS to enhance the conductivity of the electrode and also expand the active surface area for better miRNA capture efficacy. To the best of our knowledge, this unique combination (Fe-Ni@rGO/GQD-Ag/GNS) has not been employed for electrochemical biosensing of miRNA so far.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The basis of the sensor performance
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the schematic of the components of the electrochemical miR-155 nanocomposite biosensor, along with the detection mechanism of the proposed nanobiosensors. As shown in Fig. 1, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with the novel nanocomposite of Fe-Ni@rGO/GQD-Ag/GNS to enhance the sensitivity of miR-155 detection. A set of optimization experiments were carried out to evaluate different concentrations and combinations to reach the highest conductivity of the modified electrode. To ensure obtaining the best-optimized decoration and composition. Different characterizations, both electrochemical and microscopy methods, have been employed. Afterward, the thiolated single-stranded capture probe (ssDNA probes) were immobilized using the self-assembling process of thiol groups on the surface of GNS and GQD-Ag. The immobilized ssDNA probes provide a ready-to-hybridize layer for capturing the target miRNA (miR-155), upon exposure to a sample containing it. After the presence of a double-stranded oligonucleotide on the surface of the electrode, the electrochemical label (hematoxylin) molecules will be added to the surface to accumulate the double-stranded oligos via the minor groove interaction method, which is further examined in our molecular docking studies. Our data proved that the hematoxylin was much more accumulated on the double-stranded oligos compared to the single-stranded oligos. Therefore, the proposed system could distinguish between single and double-strand DNA on the electrode surface in the final readout signal (DPV reduction peak current of hematoxylin) and detect when and how much (concentration) the target miRNA is available in the samples to make a calibration curve.

The nanomaterials, including nickel ferrite modified partially reduced graphene oxide (Fe-Ni@rGO), silver-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQD-Ag), and gold nanostars (GNS), were purchased from Ronash Technology Pars Co., Iran (www.aminbic.com). All the other materials were from products of Sigma Aldrich Co., US (www.sigmaaldrich.com). All materials were purchased in analytical grade and used as received without additional purification. Also, the tubes and glassware were autoclaved and kept isolated to stay clean. Reagents, including the buffers for probe incubation, hybridization, and washing, were made and sterilized based on the protocol explained in previous studies [28,54]. All the buffers were prepared weekly with fresh pure and sterilized double-distilled water.

The oligonucleotides, including target miRNA, one-base mismatched target, three-base mismatched target, non-complementary oligo, and thiolated capture probe, were purchased from Bioneer Co., South Korea. They all were in lyophilized powder form and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade purified, in which the capture probe only had a thiol modification (SH) at the 5ʹ end. The sequence of the target miR-155 was taken from the miRBase database (www.mirbase.org), and the capture probe was designed to be completely complementary to the target miRNA.

The conventional three-electrode system was used for electrochemical measurement and characterizations. The working electrode was a GCE with a diameter of 2.0 mm, while the reference and counter electrodes were Ag/AgCl and platinum, respectively. They were connected to the potentiostat/galvanostat instrument RADstat-10 from Kianshar Danesh Co., Iran.

The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was done in a 1.0 M phosphate buffer solution with pH 7.0. The DPV measured between ‏0.35–0 V with 50 mV step potential, 25 mV amplitude, and 0.05 s modulation time. However, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) was done in a solution of 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl.

All the fabrication parameters were assessed at first in a series of experiments to determine the best and optimum values and achieve higher sensitivity for the nanobiosensor. The fabrication process started with cleaning the GCE surface by smooth polishing on a cloth soaked in a 1.0 µm and 0.05 µm alumina–water slurry. Then, the electrode was washed with double-distilled water (ddH2O) and then sonicated in water and ethanol, followed by drying in the open air. The prepared bare electrode was then modified with nanomaterials in a step-way process which was started by dropping 2.0 μL of Fe-Ni@rGO solution (30.0 mg.mL−1 in H2O) on the electrode until relatively dried. Then, a drop of 2.0 μL GQD-Ag solution (10.0 mg.mL−1 in H2O) was dropped and kept until dried, and afterward, the 2.0 μL of GNSs solutions (25.0 µg.mL−1 in H2O) was similarly applied on the electrode surface. All three used nanomaterials were firstly subjected to mild ultra-sonication for 2–5 min before each usage. Afterward, the nano-modified electrode was washed by ddH2O to rinse the unattached nanomaterials from the surface.

In the next step, the 4.0 μL of immobilization buffer solution containing 1.8 µM capture-probes was applied to the nano-modified GCE surface and incubated in a high-humidity chamber for 120 min at room temperature for self-assembled attachment of the capture-probes onto the GNSs surface. Finally, the electrode was washed with ddH2O and then immersed in a solution of 1.0 µM of 6-Mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) for 5 min and washed again with ddH2O [28]. In this step, the proposed nanobiosensor is ready to be used in hybridization with target miR-155.

For hybridization experiments, a 4.0 μL drop of hybridization solution containing target miR-155 in the desired concentration was positioned on the modified electrode for 60 min [28] and then washed with the washing solution. Afterward, the hybridized nanobiosensor was immersed in the 1.0 mM hematoxylin solution for 5 min to let the hematoxylin molecules accumulate on oligonucleotides and act as the electrochemical label [54]. Finally, the DPV measurement was done to record the reduction of peak currents of hematoxylin molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nanomaterials and electrode surface
ce:section.ce:para: To control the efficacy of each step of electrode modification, a variety of conventional characterization techniques were used. The CV and EIS analysis were performed for bare GCE and after each modification step (in the 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl) to assess the electrochemical behavior of the electrode. Also, to assess the nano-modified electrode, the electron microscope imaging using scanning electron microscope (SEM) using SIGMA VP FE-SEM and atomic force microscopy (AFM) using Brisk AFM instruments. In addition, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used for elemental analysis of the electrode surface using the SEM instrument equipped with EDS. The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed by the PerkinElmer instrument.

Molecular docking analysis can provide a better insight into the binding affinity of hematoxylin with single- and double-stranded oligonucleotides, as well as its preferable binding orientation. To identify the favorable binding conformation and their binding priority, we carried out molecular docking analysis similar to our previous publication [29]. In brief, an induced docking algorithm 1000 random poses was examined using the Molecular Operating Environment (MOE) program (Chemical Computing Group. Inc, Montreal, Canada). The atomic interactions between all the atoms were calculated based on Amber10-EHT, and Molecular Orbital PACkage (MOPAC) code was used to calculate the charges for the hematoxylin. The docking method was based on a triangle matcher algorithm in which possible binding poses were identified by the alignment of three hematoxylin atoms with three alpha spheres. The identified binding configuration was scored at first using London functions and then sorted based on GBVI/WAS (Generalized-Born Volume Integral/Weighted Surface area) was used for the second refinement. Geometric imperfections, the number of hydrogen bonds, ligand’s distortion, and desolvation all affect the LONDON calculation, while the electrostatic solvation of ligand and receptor and their accessible surface area of each impact the level of GBVI/WSA.

All the measurements were performed in three replications, and the mean value and standard deviation were calculated. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) via GraphPad Prism 8.0 software. Significance levels were presented as *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ****p < 0.0001.

Optimization of the fabrication process is the key to achieving higher sensitivity for detection in electrochemical nanobiosensors. In order to determine the optimal concentrations of nanomaterials (rGO, GQD, GNS) and to find the best combination of nanomaterials for the modification of the electrode, a series of experiments were carried out using CV in [Fe(CN)6] 3-/4- solution. The peak currents were plotted versus the method (different combinations of nanomaterials) or concentrations of nanomaterials, and the highest peak current was considered the best response. In addition, the optimum concentration of the single-stranded capture probes was considered the lower peak current since negatively-charged oligonucleotides cover the electrode surface. The results of optimization experiments are shown in Fig. 2 for the combination method of nanomaterials, different concentrations of rGO, GQD, and GNSs, and finally, the concentration of capture probes. The optimum values of each optimization experiment were used for the nanobiosensor fabrication process, which is explained in Section 2.4. In addition, the corresponding CV curves of each chart are illustrated in Supplementary Fig. S1.

As shown in Fig. 2, the peak current of bare GCE is reported for better comparison and showing the effect of nanomaterials. Fig. 2A represents the effect of three nanomaterials used for electrode modification on conductivity to enhance the sensitivity of the biosensing mechanism. In Fig. 2A (left), the highest peak current was achieved for 30.0 mg.mL−1 of rGO, and thus this was selected as the best concentration to make the nanobiosensor for further analysis. Taking a similar concept into account, the best concentration of GQD was 10.0 mg.mL−1, Fig. 2A (middle), and for GNSs best concentration was 25.0 µg.mL−1, Fig. 2A (right). As it can be concluded from Fig. 2A, adding the nanomaterials the conductivity of the electrode was significantly increased compared to the bare electrode. Next, the results of the combination of those nanomaterials showed in Fig. 2B. As it can be seen, the strategy in which the rGO, GQD, and GNS were added layer by layer (red bar) shows a higher peak current, and therefore it is selected for the GCE modification. This strategy significantly increases the peak current compared to the bare GCE and shows the significance of using nanomaterials in the improvement of nanobiosensors. Also, the higher peak current compared to rGO modified GCE (blue bar) and also rGO and GQD composite (yellow bar) show the importance of adding GNS for peak current enhancement. In addition, the layer-by-layer strategy was also better than the composite of rGO and GQD together and then adding the layer of GNS (green bar) and mixing all nanomaterials at first and then modifying the electrode with (pink bar). Overall, the layer-by-layer addition of rGO at first, then GQD, and finally GNS (red bar) was used to fabricate the proposed electrochemical nanobiosensor. Fig. 2C demonstrates the difference between concentrations of the thiolated capture SS-Probes on peak currents in which 1.8 µM was recognized as the best optimum value for fabrication of the nanobiosensor.

Characterization of step modification of the electrode using CV and EIS analysis are shown in Fig. 3. In addition, the characterization of the purchased nanomaterials and nanomaterials used on the electrode surface is shown in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterizations using CV and EIS
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution are shown in Fig. 3A to compare their peak currents and potentials. As shown, curve a is for the bare GCE electrode, and the peak current increases after modifying the electrode surface with nanomaterials (curve b). This is mainly because the combination of used nanomaterials (rGO/GQD/GNS) enhances the conductivity and expands the GCE electrode surface. By immobilizing the SS-Probes on the GNSs (curve c), the peak current dropped, and peak potential shifted to the higher potentials. After hybridizing the SS-Probes with target miR-155 (curve d), the peak current further decreased, and the potential shifted further to the positive potential. This can be attributed to the negative charge of the oligonucleotides (both SS-Probes and target miRNAs) that can repel the negative anions of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- from the electrode surface, and also the electrode surface filling by probes and miRNA decreases the chance of electrons to reach the electrode surface. To further evaluate the stepwise modification of the electrode for fabrication of the nanobiosensor, the EIS study was performed too and reported as Nyquist plot in Fig. 3B. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) difference between curves can be observed in the plot, in which the nanocomposite modified electrode (curve b) had a lower resistance, even lower than the bare electrode (curve a). However, after adding the SS-Probe (curve c) the resistance was increased, and a further increase in resistance was achieved after the addition of target miRNA (curve d). This might be due to the low conductivity as well as the hybridized oligos with negative charges repelling the anions of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, which caused higher Rct. Overall, the results of the EIS study totally agreed with the results of CV experiments, and both confirmed the step modification process of the nanobiosensor fabrication. After adding the hematoxylin to the double stranded oligoes on the electrode (curve e), the peak current and voltage in CV and the resistance in EIS did not change significantly. Minor changes could be because hematoxylin are small molecules and interact with the oligoes, but not the electrode itself.

The results of characterizations of nanomaterials and electrode surface modifications are reported here. The results of TEM imaging (Fig. 4A) show that the size (about 60 nm diameter) and star-like shape of the GNS agree with the product label and are not forming any aggregation. The FTIR spectroscopy of the nanomaterials and their layer-by-layer composition are also done, and the results are shown in Fig. 4B. The comparison of the combined sample (all three nanomaterials) with each one revealed the mechanism of their binding, which is more physical also with some weak chemical bindings between them in the layer-by-layer assembly on the electrode.

The SEM images of the modified electrode surfaces are shown in Fig. 4C for rGO modified GCE (GCE/rGO) and Fig. 4D for the GCE modified with all the nanomaterials rGO/GNS/GQD. Comparing these two figures reveals that the decoration of the GNSs is well dispersed without aggregation and covers all the electrode area to expand the functional surface. The porosity of the electrode surface is also expanded further the electrode surface and is beneficial to the sensitivity of the nanobiosensor. These observations are supported by the CV results of the electrode, which will be explained. The EDS analysis of the electrode surface is shown in Fig. 4E for GCE/rGO and Fig. 4F for GCE/rGO/GQD/GNS to report the elements available on the electrode surface. Fig. 4E shows the elements in the rGO which confirm the presence of rGO on the electrode surface. However, comparing the two plots and their element reporting tables reveals many gold atoms on the electrode surface after modifying the electrode with the GNSs. Similarly, the presence of silver atoms in low quantity shows the presence of the GQD-Ag, but the GNSs mostly cover them as in the electrode modification steps. Also, a lower amount of iron and oxygen shows the coverage of the rGO surface by GNSs.

The AFM analysis shows the electrode surface property, which was performed for the nano-modified electrode (Fig. 4G(I)), capture probe modified-nanomaterials electrode (single-stranded oligonucleotides) (Fig. 4G(II)), and the hybridized electrode-nanomaterials (double-stranded oligonucleotides) (Fig. 4G(III)). Comparing these three figures revealed that the roughness (both average roughness and root mean square (RMS) roughness) of the electrode surface is increased by adding the capture probe compared to the only nanomaterials and further increased by double-strand hybridization. This can be explained by the coverage of the electrode surface by capture probes and their orientation form formed by self-assembling immobilizing thiolated capture probes on the GNS surfaces. By hybridizing the capture probe, the spaces are further increased between immobilized capture probes by the spatial configuration of the double-stranded oligos on the electrode surface. Also, the capture probe immobilization and hybridization with target miRNA increase the average height of the electrode modification, which can also be explained by immobilizing the single-stranded capture probes and their hybridization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and detection efficacy of the nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the developed nanobiosensor was tested by comparing the DPV peak current of target miR-155 to some non-complementary oligos. Fig. 5A represents the peak current of miR-155 versus one-base different miR-155, three-base different miR-155, completely non-complementary oligo, and a mixture of miR-155 with all the non-complementary oligos. Also, Fig. 5A shows the DPV peak of SS-Probe (capture probes) for better comparison. Their respective DPV curves are also shown in Fig. 5B for better illustration. As it can be concluded from Fig. 5A and B, the peak current of the target miRNA measured by the nanobiosensor is much higher than the non-complementary oligos, revealing the selectivity of the nanobiosensor towards the target miRNA. In the mixture of target and non-complementary oligos, the peak current differs from the solution with only target miR-155, which shows the low interference of the non-complementary oligos. Also, to emphasize the role of hematoxylin as the label, the DPV of the target miRNA in PBS buffer without the use of hematoxylin is shown in Fig. 5B, which shows the peak currents are associated with the electrochemical reduction of accumulated hematoxylin molecules on the double-stranded oligos.

Quantification efficacy of the nanobiosensor for different concentrations of target miR-155 was assessed using DPV in PBS buffer, measuring reduction peak current of hematoxylin molecules assembled on double-stranded oligos (SS-Probes and miR-155). The results revealed that in a wide range of miR-155 concentrations, the relation of each concentration versus their respective peak current is linear. The histograms of peak currents in the linear range are shown in Fig. 5C, and their DPV curves are shown in Fig. 5D. Also, the calibration curve of the nanobiosensor for the linear range concentrations is shown in Fig. 5E.

In Fig. 5C and D, the curve and peak current of the SS-Probes are also reported for better comparison. Error bars in Fig. 5C are shown for three replications, and their low quantity represents the high reproducibility of the nanobiosensor. In Fig. 5E, the represented logarithmic regression function shows high sensitivity, and the regression value is near 100% (99.4%). Therefore, the linear range (dynamic range) of the nanobiosensor is from 0.05 fM to 50.0 pM of miR-155. Also, the limit of detection (LOD) of the nanobiosensor was calculated 20.2 aM, based on the method explained previously [24]. It must be mentioned that despite the high sensitivity of the proposed nanobiosensor, the peak current is still very high, and lower concentrations of the target miRNA were also considered in our tests, but their low quantity makes the solution-making process very challenging, and also the repeatability of the tests was not approved by team member, we did not report them in this paper. However, the current linear range is still in a range of medical needs and can be used in medical diagnosis. Arabkari et al. compared different miRNAs associated with breast cancer with absolute quantification by real-time PCR. Based on their report, control samples had equal to 16.6 fM of miRNA, and patients had 49.8 fM, which indicates that our nanobiosensor works to detect breast cancer patients [55].

In Table 1, the analytical performance of the current nanobiosensor is compared with previous research studies on miRNA detection for miR-155 and other miRNAs. The low LOD and wide dynamic range of the proposed nanobiosensor are superior to others reported nanobiosensors for miR-155 and other miRNAs. Furthermore, our method does not use expensive and complex materials and fabrication methods compared to most miRNA nanobiosensors. Comparing the nanobiosensor result with previously published miR-155 biosensor from our group without using nanomaterials (LOD=13.5 pM) [23], the importance of using nanomaterials in biosensors for enhancing the sensitivity is obvious. Furthermore, the type of nanomaterial can be very important too, which is evident by the other previous nano-based biosensor for miR-155 using graphene oxide and gold nanorods (LOD=0.6 fM) [28]. Also, in another publication of ours, another electrochemical miRNA nanobiosensor made of reduced graphene oxide and gold nanowires achieved a LOD of 1.7 fM [24]. We have also used magnetic Fe3O4 @Ag core-shell nanoparticles for electrochemical miR-155 detection with a LOD of 0.15 fM [29].

The electrochemical label was different between the current study and our previous reports about the nanobiosensor of miRNA. However, the main reason for significant sensitivity enhancement is the type of nanomaterials with the layer-by-layer assembling of Fe-Ni@rGO/GQD-Ag/GNSs. The modification of rGO and GQDs with conductive atoms like Fe, Ni, and Ag can help make the composite much more conducive. In addition, the special star shape of gold nanoparticles helped the conductivity and increased the surface area and, therefore, a number of the SS-Probes that can be assembled onto the electrode to capture the miRNAs. The significant enhancement in conductivity due to the incorporation of nanomaterials was also confirmed in our CV studies. The high sensitivity and selectivity of the biosensor can also be partly associated with hematoxylin as the electrochemical label, which can clearly make a difference between single-stranded and double-stranded oligos (Fig. S2). Overall, due to the utilization of the unique combination of nanomaterials, the LOD of the current nanobiosensor was much better than both the biosensor and the nanobiosensors of miR-155 developed by our team previously. In addition, in comparison with the conventional miRNA quantification methods such as RT-PCR, the presented nanobiosensor is inexpensive, fast, more sensitive, and selective [23–25].

Furthermore, the accuracy of the nanobiosensor, repeatability, and reproducibility showed enough promise for a newly developed analytical method. Five replications in the preparation of the nanobiosensor, all on different days but using the exact same procedure, demonstrated that the relative standard deviation (RSD percentage) was about 6.5% which is satisfactory. Also, by assessing the error bars of measurements done by the nanobiosensor for miR-155 detection in the linear range, the RSDs were between 2.2% and 6.7%.

Previous sections explained the extraordinary sensitivity and selectivity of the nanobiosensor towards the target miR-155 in a PBS buffer environment. However, to prove the proficiency of the nanobiosensor in real human serum samples besides the PBS buffer, a series of experiments have been done and explained here. The process starts with serum isolation from a healthy woman (age 30) to spike three different concentrations of the miR-155. Before and after spiking, the prepared nanobiosensor was used and reported. The recovery percentage and relative standard deviation percentage of the serum-based detections are reported in Table 2, while the peak currents and DPV curves of the experiments are shown in Fig. 6A and B, respectively.

Fig. 6 A represents the DPV peak current of the measurement in both serum and PBS for all three concentrations. According to this figure, there is no significant difference between PBS and serum in nanobiosensor efficacy. This means that the nanobiosensor can flawlessly perform the miRNA detection in a simulated real sample (serum) without significant interference from serum biomolecules or other moieties. Moreover, the serum's DPV voltammograms before spiking as well as prepared nanobiosensor before hybridizing (just capture probes) are also shown for better comparison. The results showed that the initial detection was almost zero (shown in Fig. 6A and B) without a significant difference with the capture probe, meaning that there is no miR-155 available in the serum or at least not in the detection range of the nanobiosensor. Furthermore, in serum samples, the CVs were obtained from 5.6% to 7.2%, each with three replicates, which are tabulated in Table 2.

To evaluate the type of interaction between hematoxylin and SS-Probe and miRNA, we have performed molecular docking simulations. The results are collectively reported in Fig. 7, which provides a better image of the binding sites and the interaction between the hematoxylin and the DNA atoms. The docking results (Fig. 7B) also depict the minor groove binding as the most common observed mode of hematoxylin/DNA binding.

Along with all 1000 evaluated poses, the three conformations with the strongest bind with oligonucleotides (single or double) are listed in Fig. 7C. As mentioned in the methodology part, the strength of the bind is derived from the calculated binding free energy. The conformations with lower GBVI/WSA show higher binding strength to DNA. The last column of Fig. 7C represents the distribution of calculated binding free energies for the top 20 conformations for both single and double-stranded DNA. The results proved that the binding between hematoxylin and a double-stranded DNA is significantly higher than its binding with a single-stranded system. According to the results shown in Fig. 7C, the binding positions were dispersed over the entire length of oligo sequences with a preference for minor groove sites. In a groove binding, the regular functionality of DNA is disrupted due to the presence of a flexible molecule binding along the minor or major groove of the DNA [70,71]. Fig. 7D, is the graph that compares the binding energy of the hematoxylin to single-stranded oligo vs. double-stranded oligo. According to all docking analysis, it can be concluded that the main interaction way of hematoxylin with DNA in major groove binding. This is different than the results of previous study on resveratrol-DNA interaction [29].

In this study, we successfully developed an ultrasensitive and selective electrochemical nanobiosensor using a novel combination of nanomaterials. The rGO, GQD, and GNS layer-by-layer addition successfully enhanced the electrode's electrical conductivity. The analytical performance of the method for measuring miR-155 was higher than most of the previous papers in terms of the limit of detection. This sensitivity seems to be the result of using a novel nanocomposite to enhance the analytical performance of the sensing system. Also, its response in the human serum environment was similar to the synthetic environment PBS buffer, which might be because of using a mechanically stable nanocomposite layer over the electrode and also using a good electrochemical label (hematoxylin) that redox reaction happens without interfering with redox of other biomolecules in the biological/clinical samples. Overall, besides the high sensitivity and selectivity and low interference, the lower cost and less time-consuming process are other superior qualities of the developed nanobiosensor compared to conventional methods for miRNA quantification. Therefore, we believe it can be further assessed to be used for future clinical application of serum analysis for early detection and screening of breast cancer. Furthermore, by changing the capture probe sequence, this nanobiosensor can be used to detect other miRNAs too. More in-depth experiments need to be done for further evaluation, and assessment for future commercialization.

All authors made a significant contribution to the work reported, whether that is in the conception, study design, execution, acquisition of data, analysis and interpretation, or in all these areas; took part in drafting, revising or critically reviewing the article; gave final approval of the version to be published; have agreed on submission of the article to Sensors and Actuators B:Chemical; and agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mostafa Azimzadeh was born (1984) and raised in the historical city of Iran; Yazd. After receiving his M.Sc. in Plant Breeding from the University of Tehran, he was a PhD Student in Nano-Biotechnology from 2012 at Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences and Technologies, University of Tehran,Tehran, Iran. His doctorate dissertation is about the design and fabrication of electrochemical nano biosensor to quantify specific miRNA for early detection of breast cancer, which is published in two papers so far. He has been an assistant professor at Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences and Health Services since 2016 and working in his Nanobiosensors laboratory with his students.

Dr. Ebrahim Mostafavi has so far received training at Stanford Cardiovascular Institute, Stanford University School of Medicine (PostDoc), Northeastern University (Ph.D.), Harvard Medical School (Researcher), and University of Tehran (M.Sc. and B.Sc.). His research interests revolve around the engineering and development of (nano)biomaterials, nanocarriers, and 3D in vitro models (3D bioprinted constructs, organoids, nanofibrous scaffolds, hydrogels, and vascular grafts) to create biologically complex systems for tissue engineering, regenerative/translational medicine, cancer therapy, biosensing, and infectious diseases. Dr. Mostafavi serves as Associate Editor-in-Chief of several prestigious and high-impact journals within Elsevier, Springer, Dove Medical Press, T&F, Frontiers, etc. He is also an Editorial Board Member of 25+ prestigious biomedical and materials science journals. His scholarly work comprises >150 publications with an H-index of 27 (i10-index= 70) including papers published in The Lancet family and JAMA Network journals. So far, he has contributed to writing more than 35 book chapters and edited several books.
